nativeRange.habitat: Steep current-swept outer reef slopes, typically forming large aggregations in the water column above the reef edge
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical reef drop-offs and outer reef walls

decorPreferences: plenty of live rock with hiding places
open swimming space above and in front of the reef structure

diet.notes: In the wild it feeds primarily on zooplankton in midwater. In captivity it generally adapts well to frozen and freeze-dried preparations such as mysis and vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and tends to accept dried algae and pellets. Multiple small feedings per day are commonly recommended, especially during acclimation.

avoidWith: very boisterous tankmates that may intimidate newly introduced specimens

summary: The Pyramid Butterflyfish (Hemitaurichthys polylepis) is widely regarded as one of the most reef-safe and beginner-friendly butterflyfishes, valued for its peaceful temperament and planktivorous diet. Best kept in larger systems with open swimming space and brisk water flow.
careNotes: The Pyramid Butterflyfish is widely traded as the 'Yellow Pyramid Butterflyfish' for its distinctive white-and-yellow body with a dark face. It is commonly regarded as one of the hardiest and most beginner-friendly butterflyfishes, since unlike most members of the butterflyfish family (Chaetodontidae) it feeds on zooplankton rather than coral polyps. In the wild it forms large midwater schools above current-swept outer reef slopes, so in captivity it benefits from open swimming space, brisk flow, and a tank with a long footprint rather than a tall, narrow one. Specimens are often shy on arrival and benefit from plenty of live-rock hiding places and several small feedings per day during acclimation. Generally considered reef-safe, though specimens have occasionally been reported to nip at Xenia and other soft corals if underfed. Butterflyfishes in this genus are commonly reported to form monogamous pairs in the wild, and aquarists tend to keep them singly, as bonded pairs, or in small groups of three or more; keeping just two unrelated individuals is generally discouraged, as this pairing tends to lead to aggression between them. Like many reef fish kept long-term, they have been reported to be susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when diet or water quality is poor, so a varied diet with marine algae and vitamin-enriched foods is generally advised.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to form pairs during spawning and to be oviparous (pelagic eggs), but captive breeding is not commonly documented in home aquaria.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source; gave family, peaceful/reef-safe rating, water parameters (72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12), and a 125 US gal minimum tank size.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Chaetodontidae, Acanthuriformes), authoritative native range (Pacific Ocean: Christmas Island to Hawaii, Line, Pitcairn, south Japan, New Caledonia — corrects LiveAquaria's 'Indian Ocean' phrasing), max length 18 cm TL, wild temperature 24.7-28.9°C, planktivorous diet, and pair-forming spawning behavior.

additional.name: ReefApp encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (18 cm), tank size (around 500 L minimum), reef-safe rating (with luck — occasional coral nipping), salinity (around SG 1.026), and confirmation that the species benefits from ample open swimming space.
